title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about the ocean and marine life. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Beneath the Blue
content.subtitle: Why the ocean remains science's greatest frontier
content.text:
The ocean covers more than seventy percent of the Earth's surface, yet remains one of the least explored environments on the planet. [31]More people have walked on the surface of the moon than have descended to the deepest point of the ocean, a statistic that reflects not merely the practical difficulty of deep-sea exploration but the extent to which human curiosity has historically been directed upward rather than downward.[/31]

The deep ocean — defined as water below two hundred metres, where sunlight no longer penetrates — was long assumed to be largely lifeless. This assumption was overturned in 1977 with the discovery of hydrothermal vents: cracks in the ocean floor through which superheated water, rich in minerals, rises from the Earth's interior. [32]Around these vents, scientists found entire ecosystems sustained not by sunlight but by chemosynthesis — the process by which certain bacteria convert chemicals rather than light into energy. The discovery fundamentally altered scientific understanding of what life requires to exist.[/32]

The species that inhabit these extreme environments have developed remarkable adaptations. [33]Many deep-sea creatures produce their own light through a process called bioluminescence, using it to attract prey, communicate with potential mates, or confuse predators in the otherwise total darkness.[/33] Others have evolved bodies capable of withstanding pressures that would be instantly fatal to surface-dwelling animals.

Despite these discoveries, scientists estimate that fewer than twenty percent of the ocean floor has been mapped in detail, and that a significant proportion of marine species remain undiscovered. [34]The challenge is not simply one of technology — deep-sea submersibles capable of reaching the ocean floor already exist — but of funding. Ocean exploration receives a fraction of the investment directed at space exploration, despite the fact that the oceans are more accessible and more directly relevant to human welfare.[/34]

The implications of this unexplored territory extend beyond scientific curiosity. Marine ecosystems regulate the Earth's climate, produce a significant proportion of the oxygen we breathe, and support the food chains on which billions of people depend. [35]Yet pollution, rising sea temperatures, and acidification driven by carbon dioxide absorption are altering ocean chemistry at a pace that exceeds the ability of many species to adapt, threatening ecological systems that are not yet fully understood.[/35]

[36]The paradox of ocean science is that we are changing the ocean faster than we are learning about it. Species may be lost before they are discovered; ecosystems may collapse before they are understood. The ocean's mysteries may ultimately prove less remarkable than its fragility.[/36]

questions.question: What does the statistic about moon visitors reflect, according to the article?
options: A) The tendency of human interest and resources to be directed more toward space than the deep ocean.
B) The greater technical difficulty of ocean exploration compared to space travel.
C) The limited resources available for all forms of scientific exploration.
D) The superior achievements of the space exploration programme overall.
questions.explanation: The statistic reflects 'the extent to which human curiosity has historically been directed upward rather than downward,' not merely practical difficulty.

questions.question: Why was the discovery of hydrothermal vents scientifically significant?
options: A) It proved that life could survive at extreme temperatures on other planets.
B) It led directly to new technologies for studying the deep ocean floor.
C) It showed that bacteria were far more widespread than scientists had previously thought.
D) It demonstrated that life could be sustained without sunlight as an energy source.
questions.explanation: Ecosystems around the vents were 'sustained not by sunlight but by chemosynthesis,' which 'fundamentally altered scientific understanding of what life requires to exist.'

questions.question: What is the main purpose of bioluminescence for deep-sea creatures, according to the text?
options: A) To help individual animals navigate in the complete absence of any natural light.
B) To warn other members of the same species of approaching danger.
C) To serve a range of functions including hunting, communication, and defence.
D) To generate warmth in the extremely cold conditions of the deep sea.
questions.explanation: The text lists three distinct purposes: attracting prey, communicating with mates, and confusing predators — a range of functions rather than a single one.

questions.question: What does the article identify as the main obstacle to ocean exploration?
options: A) The lack of technology capable of reaching the deepest parts of the ocean.
B) Insufficient financial investment compared to what space exploration receives.
C) The reluctance of governments to support marine scientific research.
D) The extreme danger posed to researchers by conditions in the deep sea.
questions.explanation: The challenge is 'not simply one of technology' since submersibles already exist, but 'of funding,' as ocean exploration receives far less investment than space exploration.

questions.question: What threat to marine ecosystems does the article highlight?
options: A) Environmental changes are occurring more rapidly than many marine species can adapt to.
B) Human fishing practices have already destroyed the majority of deep-sea habitats.
C) Rising sea levels are causing the permanent flooding of coastal marine ecosystems.
D) Scientists lack the knowledge needed to develop effective conservation strategies.
questions.explanation: The text warns that ocean chemistry is being altered 'at a pace that exceeds the ability of many species to adapt,' describing change outrunning adaptation.

questions.question: What is the central paradox described in the final paragraph?
options: A) Ocean exploration is becoming more affordable even as scientific interest in it declines.
B) Marine species are better adapted to environmental change than scientists previously believed.
C) The ocean is more thoroughly studied than other natural environments on Earth.
D) Human activity is transforming the ocean more quickly than science can study it.
questions.explanation: The paradox is stated directly: 'we are changing the ocean faster than we are learning about it,' with species and ecosystems at risk before they can be understood.
